content:
First, observe that since each \(a_i\) lies strictly between 2 and 3, any integer approximation \(A_i\) optimally will be either 2 or 3 (rounding to 1 or 4 would incur an error exceeding 1, which is far worse than what we can do with 2’s and 3’s).  

We have seven numbers, so if we chose all \(A_i=2\) the sum would be \(7\cdot2=14\), and if all \(A_i=3\) it would be \(21\).  We need the sum to be 19, i.e. \(+5\) above 14.  Each time we switch an \(A_i\) from 2 to 3 we gain 1 in the total.  Hence we must switch exactly 5 of them to 3 and leave 2 of them at 2.  

Let us write down the “errors” in each case:

For each \(i\), define  
  if \(A_i=3\), error \(=3-a_i\),  
  if \(A_i=2\), error \(=a_i-2\).  

Compute these:

i  a_i  |3–a_i|  |a_i–2|
1 2.56 0.44  0.56  
2 2.61 0.39  0.61  
3 2.65 0.35  0.65  
4 2.71 0.29  0.71  
5 2.79 0.21  0.79  
6 2.82 0.18  0.82  
7 2.86 0.14  0.86  

We must pick 5 of them to be rounded up to 3, and the remaining 2 to 2, so as to minimize the maximum error.  To minimize the worst “round‐down” error \(a_i-2\), we should choose the two smallest values of \(a_i-2\), namely for indices 1 and 2.  Thus we set

 \(A_1=2,\;A_2=2\),  (errors 0.56, 0.61)  
 and \(A_3=A_4=A_5=A_6=A_7=3\),  (errors all ≤0.44).

The largest error among these seven is \(\max(0.61,0.44)=0.61\).  Hence the minimal possible \(M\) is \(0.61\), and

\[
100M \;=\;100\cdot0.61\;=\;61.
\]

\(\boxed{61}\)

i  a_i  |3–a_i|  |a_i–2|

2 2.61 0.39  0.61  
3 2.65 0.35  0.65  
4 2.71 0.29  0.71

\(\boxed{61}\)
